title: Understanding Section 106 of the NHPA: A Complete Guide
content:
Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act is one of the most consequential federal regulations governing construction and land development in the United States. If your project involves federal funding, permitting, or land, understanding Section 106 of the NHPA is not optional—it is a legal requirement that can determine whether your project proceeds on schedule or faces costly delays.

Quick Answer: Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) requires federal agencies to consider the effects of their undertakings on historic properties before approving a project. This process involves identifying cultural resources, assessing potential impacts, and consulting with stakeholders to resolve any adverse effects.

Enacted in 1966 and codified at 54 U.S.C. § 306108, Section 106 applies to any project that requires a federal permit, license, or funding. That broad scope means the regulation touches transportation infrastructure, energy development, telecommunications, housing projects, and more. The goal is not to halt development but to ensure that historic and archaeological resources receive thoughtful consideration before irreversible decisions are made.

The regulations implementing Section 106 are found at 36 CFR Part 800, administered by the Advisory Council on Historic Preservation (ACHP). State Historic Preservation Offices (SHPOs) play a central role as consulting parties, and tribes may be involved when projects affect areas of traditional cultural significance. Understanding how these pieces fit together is essential for any project manager, developer, or federal agency navigating the compliance process.

What Projects Are Subject to Section 106 Review?

A project triggers Section 106 review whenever a federal agency has jurisdiction over an "undertaking"—a broad term covering direct federal actions, federally assisted projects, and activities requiring federal permits or approvals. If your project involves any federal nexus, Section 106 almost certainly applies.

Common examples include highway and bridge construction funded by the Federal Highway Administration, pipelines and transmission lines requiring Army Corps of Engineers permits, housing developments receiving HUD grants, and broadband infrastructure projects using federal subsidies. Even projects on private land can be subject to review if they require a federal permit such as a Section 404 dredge-and-fill authorization.

The threshold question is straightforward: does the federal government have any role in authorizing, funding, or carrying out the project? If the answer is yes, the lead federal agency is responsible for initiating the Section 106 process. Agencies cannot delegate that responsibility away, though they may rely on applicants—developers, state DOTs, utilities—to assist with the practical work of cultural resource investigations.

How Does the Section 106 Consultation Process Work?

The Section 106 consultation process follows four sequential steps established in 36 CFR Part 800: initiate the process, identify historic properties, assess effects, and resolve adverse effects. Each step has defined timelines and involves coordination among the agency, the SHPO, tribes, and other consulting parties.

Step 1: Initiate the process. The lead federal agency determines the undertaking's scope, identifies consulting parties, and plans how public involvement will be handled. The agency notifies the SHPO and any relevant Tribal Historic Preservation Offices (THPOs) of the undertaking.

Step 2: Identify historic properties. The agency defines an Area of Potential Effects (APE) and conducts—or oversees—a cultural resource survey to locate historic properties within that area. A Phase I archaeological survey is typically required for the ground-disturbing portions of the APE, while architectural historians may survey standing structures. Properties that meet the criteria for the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP) are identified and evaluated.

Step 3: Assess effects. If historic properties are present, the agency determines whether the undertaking will have an adverse effect—a finding that the project will alter the characteristics that qualify the property for the National Register. Common adverse effects include demolition, physical alteration, change of setting, introduction of visual or audible elements, and transfer of ownership.

Step 4: Resolve adverse effects. If adverse effects are found, the agency, SHPO, ACHP, and other parties negotiate a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or Programmatic Agreement (PA) that describes how adverse effects will be mitigated. Data recovery excavation, documentation, interpretive programs, or project redesign are among the common mitigation measures.

What Is the Area of Potential Effects in Section 106?

The Area of Potential Effects, or APE, is the geographic zone within which an undertaking may directly or indirectly alter the characteristics of historic properties. Defining the APE correctly is one of the most consequential early decisions in the Section 106 process, because it determines where surveys must be conducted.

The APE is not simply the project footprint. It must account for indirect effects such as visual impacts on historic viewsheds, noise and vibration from construction, changes in land use patterns, and the introduction of new elements that alter the setting of a historic district. A cell tower, for example, may have a small direct footprint but a large visual APE extending for miles.

The lead federal agency defines the APE in consultation with the SHPO, and consulting parties may challenge an APE they consider too narrow. An inadequately defined APE is a common source of project delays and legal challenges, making it critical to engage experienced cultural resource professionals early in project planning. Professional archaeological services can help define a defensible APE and design an efficient survey strategy.

What Happens When an Adverse Effect Is Found?

An adverse effect finding does not automatically stop a project. Instead, it triggers a formal consultation to develop a plan for resolving the adverse effect. This is often accomplished through a Memorandum of Agreement, a legally binding document that outlines specific mitigation commitments.

Mitigation options vary widely depending on the nature of the resource and the severity of the effect. For archaeological sites, Phase III data recovery excavations are the most common approach, using systematic excavation to recover scientific data before the site is destroyed. For historic buildings, mitigation may include detailed photographic and architectural documentation following the Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS) standards.

The ACHP has the right to participate in resolving adverse effects and may comment formally if it disagrees with the proposed resolution. Failure to complete Section 106 consultation before approving a project can expose the federal agency to legal challenges and court injunctions that halt construction. Proactive engagement with the historic preservation process is always more efficient than reactive litigation.

How Long Does Section 106 Review Take?

The timeline for Section 106 compliance depends on project complexity, the number of consulting parties, and whether adverse effects require negotiation. For straightforward projects with no historic properties in the APE, the process can conclude in a few weeks. Complex projects with large APEs, many historic properties, or contentious adverse effects can take a year or more.

The regulations set specific response deadlines: the SHPO has 30 days to respond to agency submissions at each step, and failure to respond is generally treated as concurrence. However, those timelines assume prompt submission of complete and well-documented materials. Inadequate survey reports or poorly documented effect assessments routinely generate requests for additional information that reset the clock.

Project developers can minimize delays by beginning the Section 106 process during early project planning—before alignments and designs are fixed—so that cultural resource considerations can genuinely inform project decisions. Engaging a qualified CRM firm with regional expertise and established SHPO relationships is the most effective way to keep the process on track. GIS-based survey planning using remote sensing and predictive modeling can also reduce fieldwork time by focusing investigations where sites are most likely to occur.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who is responsible for initiating Section 106 consultation?

The lead federal agency with primary jurisdiction over the undertaking is responsible for initiating Section 106 consultation. While agencies may ask applicants or project proponents to assist with the practical work of surveys and reports, the legal obligation remains with the federal agency and cannot be delegated away.

Does Section 106 apply to private development projects?

Section 106 applies to private projects whenever they involve a federal nexus—federal funding, a federal permit, or federal land. A private developer building a pipeline that requires an Army Corps of Engineers permit, for example, must comply with Section 106 even though the project is privately funded and on private land.

What is the difference between Section 106 and Section 110 of the NHPA?

Section 106 is a procedural requirement to consider effects on historic properties before approving undertakings. Section 110 imposes affirmative duties on federal agencies to identify, evaluate, and protect historic properties they own or control. Both sections work together but apply in different contexts and impose different obligations.

Can a project proceed if it will have an adverse effect on a historic property?

Yes. An adverse effect finding does not prohibit a project. It requires the agency and consulting parties to negotiate mitigation measures, typically documented in a Memorandum of Agreement. If parties cannot reach agreement, the Advisory Council on Historic Preservation may comment, but the agency retains decision-making authority to proceed.

Work With Experienced Section 106 Specialists

Navigating Section 106 of the NHPA requires technical expertise, regulatory knowledge, and established relationships with SHPOs and federal agencies. TVAR brings over 14 years of experience conducting Phase I, II, and III investigations across the Southeast, with RPA-certified archaeologists and architectural historians who understand the full compliance workflow from APE definition through MOA execution.

Whether you are a federal agency initiating consultation, a state DOT managing a corridor project, or a private developer seeking a federal permit, early engagement with qualified cultural resource professionals is the single most effective investment you can make in keeping your project on schedule. excerpt: Learn how Section 106 of the NHPA works, who it applies to, and how the consultation process protects historic properties during federally funded or permitted projects.
